briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon, everyone. I will now begin the climate briefing for April 5, 2026.

sections.title: ⚠️ Climate Change: Urgent Impacts on Natural Ecosystems and Society
sections.content: Climate change is rapidly altering the landscape of our daily lives. Domestically, the optimal tree-planting season has shifted earlier to within March, changing the traditional Arbor Day landscape [1, 3], and in the Baekdudaegan mountain range, conifers are reported to be drying out more extensively and rapidly [33]. Internationally, research has revealed that thawing permafrost is emitting significantly more greenhouse gases than anticipated [45], and French ski resorts are facing a 'downward spiral' amidst climate change and financial crises [42], indicating a global intensification of the direct impacts of the climate crisis.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Carbon Neutrality Initiatives: Strengthening Policies and Expanding Citizen Participation
sections.content: Domestically, policy efforts to achieve carbon neutrality goals are continuing. The citizen deliberation process has concluded ahead of the amendment to the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act [2], and future generations are raising questions about greenhouse gas reduction pathways [5]. Furthermore, Gumi City and Pohang City are recruiting second-round participants for the vehicle carbon neutrality points system [10, 16, 17], and carbon neutrality education programs for children are being operated in areas such as Cheongsong Hwamok Elementary School and Daejeon Daedeok-gu [9, 14, 15], demonstrating active efforts to encourage community and citizen participation.

sections.title: 🌱 Renewable Energy Transition: Expanding Efforts and Challenges Ahead
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy is continuously being pursued. This year's renewable energy budget has been increased by 42%, which is expected to have a positive impact on the related industry [24], and the industrial foundation is being strengthened with the official launch of Hanjaeyeon, a 'unified front' for renewable energy [21]. By 2030, renewable energy from the West and South Seas is planned to be supplied to the Seoul Metropolitan Area via the power grid [29], and innovative attempts are also emerging, such as Gurye County establishing a 'citizen basic income' model utilizing renewable energy [30]. However, challenges remain clear, including a 30% surge in solar power fires, urgently requiring improved management of aging facilities [25], and a tendency for some large IT companies to revert to gas-fired power generation [28].

sections.title: 🌍 International Community's Movements to Strengthen Climate Adaptation and Resilience
sections.content: The international community is pursuing plans to strengthen adaptation and resilience in response to the threat of climate change. The Dewey region in the United States plans to advance its climate change resilience plan [41], and Pittsford is also responding to climate change despite federal funding cuts [48]. Experts emphasize that coral reef science must adapt to stay ahead of climate change [38], and in Vermont, where climate adaptation costs are already accruing, there is advocacy for legislation that imposes payment obligations on fossil fuel companies [44], demonstrating the materialization of the financial burden of climate change.

briefing.closing: From the direct impacts of climate change discussed in today's briefing to domestic and international efforts for carbon neutrality, our role in achieving a sustainable future is becoming increasingly crucial. This concludes today's climate briefing for April 5, 2026.

articles.title: Conifers in Baekdudaegan Wither and Die More Widely and Rapidly
articles.original_title: 더 넓게 빠르게 말라죽는 백두대간 침엽수
articles.description: Green Korea United analyzes 'mass die-off' over 10 years. Reduced winter snowfall and high summer temperatures, among other abnormal climate conditions, are pushing Korean fir (Gusangnamu) to the brink of extinction. The phenomenon of conifers dying off in the Baekdudaegan mountain range and national park areas is accelerating. Analysis shows that some tree species have entered the extinction phase due to the impact of climate change. According to the '2026 Baekdudaegan National Park Climate Crisis Conifer Report' published by Green Korea United on the 5th, since 2015...
